meta_description: Stereolithography (SLA) 3D printing uses a UV laser to cure a thermoset resin layer by layer. The result is plastic parts with smooth surface finishes and fine features. Get an instant quote today.

sections.heading: Stereolithography (SLA) Resin 3D Printing Service
sections.text: Stereolithography (SLA) Resin 3D Printing Service Accelerate your path from concept to production with SLA. Iterate early with confidence, then transition seamlessly to production-quality parts Get Instant Quote

sections.heading: Accelerate your path from concept to production with SLA. Iterate early with confidence, then transition seamlessly to production-quality parts
sections.text: Accelerate your path from concept to production with SLA. Iterate early with confidence, then transition seamlessly to production-quality parts

sections.heading: What is Stereolithgraphy?
sections.text: What is Stereolithgraphy? Stereolithography (SLA) is a 3D printing process used to create concept models, cosmetic prototypes, and complex parts with intricate geometries. It's an additive manufacturing technology that forms parts by curing thermoset resin with a UV laser. A wide selection of materials, extremely high feature resolutions, and quality surface finishes are possible with SLA. SLA 3D printing is primarily used for: parts requiring high accuracy and features as small as 0.002 in. good surface quality for cosmetic prototypes form and fit testing

sections.heading: When to Use SLA Throughout Product Development
sections.text: When to Use SLA Throughout Product Development Use SLA across every stage of development by starting with cost-effective materials for early iteration, then progressing to production-ready resins as your design is finalized.

sections.heading: Early Stage: Concept & Iteration
sections.text: Early Stage: Concept & Iteration Use cost-effective, ABS-Like Value resin to quickly explore and refine designs: Lower-cost resin enables design iterations Fast builds support rapid concept validation Ideal for early-stage form and geometry exploration

sections.heading: Validation Stage: Form, Fit, & Function
sections.text: Validation Stage: Form, Fit, & Function Transition to higher-resolution SLA builds and improved finishes as your design stabilizes: Validate form, fit, and functional requirements Improve surface quality for realistic prototypes Prepare designs for downstream processes

sections.heading: Production Stage: End-use Parts
sections.text: Production Stage: End-use Parts Move to advanced SLA materials and finishing options once your design is finalized: Use performance-oriented resins suited for end-use Achieve consistent, repeatable part quality Apply finishing processes for production-level appearance and performance

sections.heading: How Engineers Use SLA Across the Product Lifecycle
sections.text: How Engineers Use SLA Across the Product Lifecycle Engineers rely on SLA throughout product development for its ability to deliver precise, repeatable parts, from early concept models to production-ready components.

sections.heading: Precision for Complex Geometry
sections.text: Precision for Complex Geometry Produce fine features and intricate details early in the design process while accurately capturing design intent without simplifying geometry and maintaining dimensional accuracy across iterations.

sections.heading: Surface Quality for Realistic Validation
sections.text: Surface Quality for Realistic Validation Achieve smooth surfaces for cosmetic and functional prototypes, reduce post-processing in early and mid stages, and enable more accurate evaluation of fit, finish, and appearance.

sections.heading: Material Range that Scales
sections.text: Material Range that Scales Start with cost-effective, general-purpose resins for early iteration, transition to engineering-grade materials for validation, and move to performance-oriented resins for end-use applications.

sections.heading: Repeatability from Prototype to Production
sections.text: Repeatability from Prototype to Production Ensure consistent part quality across builds, support reliable testing and validation cycles, and scale to low-volume production without changing processes.

sections.heading: Surface Finish Options for SLA Parts
sections.text: Surface Finish Options for SLA Parts Unfinished Dots, or standing "nibs," remain evident on the bottom of the part from the support structure remnants. Good for when fast lead times are required or cost is a factor Natural Supported surfaces are sanded down to eliminate the support nibs. Standard Supported surfaces are sanded, and the entire part is finely blasted for a consistent look. Note that the layers are still present. Custom Soft-touch paint, clear part finishing, painting, masking, color matching, decals/graphic, and texture finishes are available.

sections.heading: Additional Finishing Options for Stereolithography
sections.text: Additional Finishing Options for Stereolithography Custom finishing is a mix of science, technology, and fine art that can transform a part to your exact specifications. Finishes include: Soft-touch paint Clear part finishing Paint finishes Decals/graphic Texture Masking

sections.heading: Our SLA 3D Printers
sections.text: Our SLA 3D Printers Our stereolithography machines consists of Vipers, ProJets, and iPros. In high-resolution mode, Vipers and ProJets can make parts with extremely tiny features and crisp details, while in normal-resolution mode, they can build cost-effective parts very quickly. iPros have extremely large build volumes at 29 in. by 25 in. by 21 in. (736mm by 635mm by 533mm), yet are still able to image highly detailed parts easily.

sections.heading: How Does SLA 3D Printing Work?
sections.text: How Does SLA 3D Printing Work? The SLA machine begins drawing the layers of the support structures, followed by the part itself, with an ultraviolet laser aimed onto the surface of a liquid thermoset resin. After a layer is imaged on the resin surface, the build platform shifts down and a recoating bar moves across the platform to apply the next layer of resin. The process is repeated layer by layer until the build is complete. Newly built parts are taken out of machine and into a lab where solvents are used to remove any additional resins. When the parts are completely clean, the support structures are manually removed. From there, parts undergo a UV-curing cycle to fully solidify the oute...
